mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers U.S. history from 1763-1920 including the Revolution, Civil War, westward expansion, and industrialization.
Traces the Revolutionary period from colonial opposition to British taxation through the Declaration of Independence and the War of Independence (A.1.a).
Examines the Constitutional Convention, the drafting of the federal Constitution, and the struggle for ratification (A.1.b).
Hamilton's economic policies and role as a key architect of the federal government are explicitly discussed in A.1.b.ii.
Traces constitutional development from the ratification debates through the Reconstruction amendments that reshaped American governance (A.1.b, B.1).
Directly relevant to Lincoln's election (1860), the secession crisis, the Civil War, and emancipation policies (A.3).
Directly addresses the Civil War (1860-65) including sectionalism, secession, military campaigns, and emancipation (A.3).
Examines the institution of slavery, the Abolitionist movement, and the economic and psychological foundations of the slave system (A.2, A.3).
The Abolitionist movement was a driving force in antebellum reform and the sectional crisis that led to civil war (A.2.c, A.3.a).
Directly addresses Radical Reconstruction (1866-77) and the emergence of the New South, including freedmen's struggles, sharecropping, and Jim Crow (B.1).
Provides first-person perspectives on slavery, abolition, Black Codes, and Jim Crow, giving human depth to the political history (A.3, B.1).
Traces African American history through slavery, the Civil War, Reconstruction, and Jim Crow, centering the Black experience within the national narrative (A.3, B.1).
Relevant to the many presidential administrations discussed from Washington through Wilson, including their policies on expansion, reform, and war.
Relevant to congressional politics including the Radical Republican Congress, the legislative supremacy period (1877-1900), and tariff legislation (B.1, B.2.f).
Relevant to the Supreme Court under Marshall and the development of federal judicial power (A.1.b.iii, A.2.a).
Traces the formation of American political parties from the Federalist-Republican split through Whigs, Democrats, and the rise of the Republican Party (A-B).
Provides a political narrative of the United States from its founding through the Progressive Era (A-B).
Traces the development of federal law including civil rights legislation and antitrust regulation under the Sherman Act (B.1, B.2.f).
Tocqueville's analysis of American democracy is directly relevant to Jacksonian democracy and the extension of the franchise (A.2.b).
Essential for understanding Indian removal under Jackson, the violation of treaties, and the Indian wars of westward expansion (A.2.b, B.2.b.iv).
Explores the development of the American West (1865-1900) including mining, cattle ranching, railroads, and the conflicts with Native peoples (B.2.b).
Provides a regional perspective on slavery, secession, Reconstruction, and the emergence of the Jim Crow South (A.3, B.1).
Traces successive waves of immigration -- Irish and German in the 1830s-50s, then southern and eastern European -- and their impact on American society (A.2.a.iii, B.2.a).
Addresses women's roles in antebellum reform movements and the Progressive Era, including suffrage and social welfare campaigns (A.2.c, B.3.b).
Explores cultural developments from the Transcendentalist movement through the Gilded Age arts and letters (A.2.e, B.4).
Traces American intellectual life from Transcendentalism and the growth of pragmatism through Progressive-era thought (A.2.e, B.4).
Directly addresses the Progressive Movement (c. 1896-1920) including muckrakers, social reform, and the administrations of Roosevelt, Taft, and Wilson (B.3.b).
Addresses U.S. entry into World War I, military mobilization, the Western Front campaigns, and Wilson's role at the Paris Peace Conference (B.3.c).
Focuses on the Paris Peace Conference and the Senate's consequential rejection of the Treaty of Versailles (B.3.c.iii).
Provides theoretical context for understanding Jacksonian democracy, the extension of the franchise, and evolving democratic institutions (A.2.b).
Illuminates the tension between federal and state power that runs through American history from the Constitutional Convention onward (A.1.b).
Provides the economic framework for understanding industrialization, the rise of trusts and holding companies, and the manufacturing boom (B.2.c).
Places American industrialization within the broader context of the global Industrial Revolution (A.2, B.2.c).
Provides context for understanding America's emergence as an imperial power, the Spanish-American War, and the acquisition of overseas territories (B.3.a).
Traces Canadian history from British colonial rule through Confederation (1867) and subsequent national development (C).
Addresses the naval dimensions of the Revolution, the War of 1812, Civil War riverine and coastal campaigns, and the Spanish-American War.
Provides military history spanning the Revolution, War of 1812, Mexican War, Civil War, Indian wars, and Spanish-American War.
Traces the growth of American business from railroads and trusts through the corporate economy of the Gilded Age (B.2.c-d).
Provides context for U.S.-Mexican relations including the Mexican War (1846-48) and Wilson's intervention in Mexico's revolution (A.2.d, B.3.b.iii).
Traces American foreign policy from the Monroe Doctrine through the Open Door policy and Roosevelt Corollary to entry into World War I (B.3.a, B.3.c).
American poetry from Whitman through the moderns reflects the cultural currents discussed in A.2.e and B.4.a.
The temperance movement was a major reform activity in the Age of Reform (1830-50) and the Progressive Era (A.2.c, B.3.b).
Addresses Asian immigration and anti-Chinese sentiment in the context of westward expansion and exclusionary immigration policies (B.2).
Provides theoretical background for understanding the growth of American nationalism, the Era of Good Feelings, and the rise of sectionalism (A.2.a).
Addresses diaspora communities and waves of immigration that reshaped American demographics and culture (A.2.a.iii, B.2.a).
Addresses the racial dimensions of slavery, Jim Crow, white supremacy, and anti-immigrant prejudice that shaped American society (A.3, B.1, B.2.a).
